tests.title: IELTS Academic Reading — Test 01
tests.description: Original full-length Academic Reading test: 3 passages, 40 questions, 60 minutes.

sections.title: Passage 1 — The Secret Life of Soil
sections.instructions: You should spend about 20 minutes on Questions 1–13.
sections.passage:
The Secret Life of Soil

¶1 Beneath our feet lies one of the planet's least understood ecosystems. To most people soil is simply dirt, an inert backdrop to the more visible drama of plants and animals above it. In reality a single teaspoon of healthy soil contains more living organisms than there are people on Earth, and the overwhelming majority of them have never been formally described by science. Far from being lifeless, soil ranks among the most densely populated habitats known, and the creatures within it are organised into food webs every bit as intricate as those of a rainforest or a coral reef. The difference is simply that this drama unfolds in darkness, on a scale too small for the human eye to follow.

¶2 The inhabitants of this hidden world range from bacteria and other single-celled organisms to fungi, mites, beetles and earthworms. Together they drive the slow but essential process of decomposition, breaking down fallen leaves, dead roots and animal remains into the dark, crumbly material known as humus. Earthworms alone can pass enormous quantities of soil through their bodies, mixing and aerating it as they go. Without this constant recycling, the nutrients locked inside dead matter would never be returned to the ground, and the plants that depend on them would quickly fail. Decomposition, in other words, is the quiet engine that keeps the entire system running, and the fertility of farmland rests almost entirely upon it. A field stripped of its decomposers is, biologically speaking, already dying, however green and productive it may appear from the road.

¶3 Among the most remarkable of these organisms are the mycorrhizal fungi, which form an intimate partnership with the roots of most plants. The fungi extend a vast web of microscopic threads through the soil, drawing in water and minerals that they deliver to the plant; in return they receive sugars produced by photosynthesis. Recent research suggests that these fungal networks can even link neighbouring plants, allowing them to exchange nutrients and, some scientists argue, chemical warnings of disease or insect attack. This underground network, sometimes nicknamed the wood wide web, is forcing biologists to reconsider how far plants cooperate rather than simply compete for light and space.

¶4 Soil also performs a service of global importance: it stores carbon. The world's soils together hold more carbon than the atmosphere and all living plants combined. When organic matter is buried and broken down slowly, much of the carbon it contains can remain locked underground for centuries. Healthy, undisturbed soil is therefore one of the planet's largest and most dependable carbon stores, and protecting it has quietly become a priority in the wider effort to slow climate change. Disturb that soil, however, and the stored carbon can escape back into the air with surprising speed. Ploughing a long-undisturbed field, for instance, exposes buried organic matter to oxygen, and microbes then convert it rapidly into carbon dioxide, so that careless land management can turn a carbon store into a carbon source almost overnight.

¶5 Yet soil is under threat as never before. Intensive agriculture, with its heavy ploughing and its reliance on chemical fertilisers, strips the land of organic matter and leaves it exposed to erosion. Each year wind and rain carry away billions of tonnes of topsoil, frequently from the very fields that are meant to feed us. Because fertile soil forms extraordinarily slowly, with a single centimetre sometimes taking a century or more to develop, these losses cannot easily be reversed. In some regions farmers are, in effect, mining a resource that will not be replaced within their lifetimes, or even within those of their grandchildren.

¶6 A growing number of farmers, however, are adopting methods designed to rebuild the soil rather than deplete it. Planting cover crops between harvests shields the bare ground and adds organic matter when the plants are later turned back in. Reducing or abandoning ploughing, an approach known as no-till farming, leaves the soil's delicate structure and its communities of organisms undisturbed. Spreading compost returns nutrients and feeds the microbial life below. Taken together these techniques are often described as regenerative agriculture, and a growing body of evidence suggests they can restore fertility while still producing healthy harvests.

¶7 The lesson emerging from this research is that soil should be treated not as dirt but as a form of living infrastructure, as essential as any road, pipeline or power cable. It feeds us, filters our water and helps to regulate the climate, yet it can be ruined far faster than it can ever be rebuilt. Whether the ground beneath our feet remains a thriving ecosystem or dwindles into lifeless dust will depend, in the end, on decisions being taken on farms and in laboratories right now. For a substance so easily taken for granted, the stakes could hardly be higher, since almost every meal that reaches a human table ultimately begins in the thin layer of living earth that covers the land.

questions.text: A teaspoon of healthy soil can contain more organisms than the human population of the planet.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 1 states a teaspoon of healthy soil holds 'more living organisms than there are people on Earth', so the statement is supported.

evidence.quote: more living organisms than there are people on Earth

questions.text: Most of the organisms living in soil have already been described by scientists.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 1 says the 'overwhelming majority of them have never been formally described by science', which contradicts the claim that most have been described.

evidence.quote: have never been formally described by science

questions.text: Soil food webs are simpler than those found in rainforests.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 1 says soil food webs are 'every bit as intricate as those of a rainforest or a coral reef', so calling them simpler contradicts the text.

evidence.quote: every bit as intricate as those of a rainforest or a coral reef

questions.text: Earthworms help to mix and aerate the soil.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 says earthworms pass soil through their bodies, 'mixing and aerating it as they go'.

evidence.quote: mixing and aerating it as they go

questions.text: Soil holds less carbon than the atmosphere alone.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 4 states the world's soils hold 'more carbon than the atmosphere and all living plants combined', so 'less than the atmosphere alone' is false.

evidence.quote: more carbon than the atmosphere and all living plants combined

questions.text: Governments have agreed international targets for protecting soil carbon.
questions.explanation: Although Paragraph 4 mentions soil carbon and climate change, it says nothing about international targets, so this cannot be confirmed.

questions.text: Dead leaves and roots are broken down into a dark material called ______.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 says decomposition breaks dead matter into 'the dark, crumbly material known as humus'.

evidence.quote: the dark, crumbly material known as humus

questions.text: In their partnership with plants, mycorrhizal fungi receive ______ created by photosynthesis.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 3 explains that in return for water and minerals the fungi 'receive sugars produced by photosynthesis'.

evidence.quote: they receive sugars produced by photosynthesis

questions.text: Avoiding ploughing is an approach known as ______ farming.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 6 names the practice of reducing or abandoning ploughing 'an approach known as no-till farming'.

evidence.quote: an approach known as no-till farming

questions.text: Which paragraph mentions a nickname for the network formed by fungi?
questions.explanation: Paragraph 3 calls the fungal network the 'wood wide web'.

evidence.quote: sometimes nicknamed the wood wide web

questions.text: Which paragraph explains how slowly fertile soil is created?
questions.explanation: Paragraph 5 notes that 'a single centimetre sometimes taking a century or more to develop'.

evidence.quote: a single centimetre sometimes taking a century or more to develop

questions.text: Which paragraph compares soil to built infrastructure such as roads?
questions.explanation: Paragraph 7 argues soil is 'a form of living infrastructure, as essential as any road, pipeline or power cable'.

evidence.quote: as essential as any road, pipeline or power cable

questions.text: What term describes farming methods intended to rebuild the soil?
questions.explanation: Paragraph 6 says these techniques 'are often described as regenerative agriculture'.

evidence.quote: often described as regenerative agriculture

sections.title: Passage 2 — Designing the Walkable City
sections.instructions: You should spend about 20 minutes on Questions 14–26.
sections.passage:
Designing the Walkable City

¶1 For much of the twentieth century, the car was the hero of urban planning. Cities on every continent were rebuilt around the needs of the motorist, with wide roads driven through old neighbourhoods, generous car parks attached to every building, and homes pushed ever further from the places where people worked and shopped. Planners of the era regarded this transformation as obvious progress. The car promised speed, freedom and status, and a city designed around it seemed, almost by definition, a modern one. Engineers competed to build faster ring roads and taller flyovers, and the very language of planning filled with talk of traffic flow and vehicle capacity. Pedestrians, where they were considered at all, were treated as an obstacle to be channelled safely out of the way of the moving traffic.

¶2 The costs of that vision took decades to become fully visible. As distances grew, walking and cycling ceased to be practical for everyday journeys, and residents found themselves dependent on a vehicle for even the smallest errand. Traffic congestion, air pollution and the steady loss of public space followed. Perhaps most striking was the effect on street life: the lively pavements and corner shops of the older city gave way to roads that were efficient to drive through but unpleasant to linger in. A place built for cars, critics began to argue, was rarely a place built for people. Children, who had once played and met their friends in the street, were increasingly confined indoors or driven everywhere by anxious parents. The elderly and the poor, who were less likely to own a car, often found whole districts of their own city effectively closed to them.

¶3 In response, a growing movement of planners has called for cities to be designed once again around the pedestrian. The central idea is captured in the phrase the fifteen-minute city, an arrangement in which most of the things a resident needs, shops, schools, clinics, parks and workplaces, lie within a short walk or cycle of home. Rather than zoning each district for a single purpose, this approach mixes homes, businesses and services together, so that daily life can be conducted without a car. Its supporters present it less as a radical experiment than as a return to how cities functioned for most of their history. Before the motor age, after all, almost every settlement was a walkable one, simply because there was no alternative; the dense, mixed neighbourhood was not a clever invention but the ordinary default, repeated in market towns and capital cities alike.

¶4 The benefits claimed for walkable design are wide-ranging. Residents who walk or cycle as part of their routine tend to be healthier, and the reduction in traffic cuts both pollution and the emissions that drive climate change. Local shops, freed from competition with distant retail parks, are more likely to survive, keeping money circulating within the neighbourhood. There are social gains too. When people move through their area on foot, they encounter their neighbours, and streets that are busy with pedestrians tend to feel safer than those given over entirely to traffic. Quieter streets also tend to be greener, since space once swallowed by parking can be returned to trees and planting, which in turn cool the air during heatwaves and soak up rainwater that would otherwise overwhelm the drains.

¶5 Achieving this vision, however, is far from straightforward. Many cities have spent so long building outwards that reversing the pattern is slow and expensive. Roads must be narrowed, pavements widened and public transport extended, all of which require money and political courage. Residents accustomed to driving may resist changes that appear to inconvenience them, even when those changes would benefit the wider community. Planners have learned that imposing a walkable design from above, without persuading the people who must live with it, is a reliable way to provoke opposition. Successful schemes, by contrast, tend to begin small, with a single reclaimed square or a handful of quietened streets, allowing residents to experience the benefits for themselves before committing to anything larger. Trust, planners have found, is built one block at a time.

¶6 There is also a risk that the benefits will be shared unequally. When a district is made more attractive, with leafy streets and pleasant squares, property prices often rise, and the very residents the improvements were meant to serve can find themselves priced out. Critics warn that walkability, pursued carelessly, may become a luxury enjoyed mainly by the wealthy, while poorer families are pushed to the car-dependent edges of the city. For this reason, thoughtful planners now treat affordable housing as an essential companion to walkable design rather than an afterthought.

¶7 None of these difficulties has dimmed the movement's appeal. A number of cities, from Paris to Melbourne, have begun reclaiming road space for pedestrians and cyclists, and early results have been encouraging. The deeper significance of the shift may be philosophical rather than practical. After a century in which the city was shaped to suit the machine, planners are once again asking a simpler question: what kind of place do human beings actually want to live in? The walkable city is, above all, an attempt to answer it. The car will not disappear, and few campaigners seriously wish it to; the aim is balance rather than banishment, a city in which walking is once again the natural and obvious choice for the shortest everyday journeys, and the car simply one option among several rather than the only one a sensible person would consider.

questions.text: Heading for Paragraph 1
options: An idea rooted in the cities of the past
The hidden price of car-centred design
The danger of benefits for the few
A question that outlives the practical details
questions.explanation: Paragraph 1's main idea is that planning was built around the car: 'the car was the hero of urban planning'.

evidence.quote: the car was the hero of urban planning

questions.text: Heading for Paragraph 2
options: An idea rooted in the cities of the past
The hidden price of car-centred design
The danger of benefits for the few
A question that outlives the practical details
correct_answer: The hidden price of car-centred design
questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 describes the costs that 'took decades to become fully visible' — congestion, pollution and lost street life.

evidence.quote: The costs of that vision took decades to become fully visible

questions.text: Heading for Paragraph 3
options: An idea rooted in the cities of the past
The hidden price of car-centred design
The danger of benefits for the few
A question that outlives the practical details
correct_answer: An idea rooted in the cities of the past
questions.explanation: Paragraph 3 presents the fifteen-minute city as 'a return to how cities functioned for most of their history'.

evidence.quote: a return to how cities functioned for most of their history

questions.text: Heading for Paragraph 4
options: An idea rooted in the cities of the past
The hidden price of car-centred design
The danger of benefits for the few
A question that outlives the practical details
questions.explanation: Paragraph 4 lists benefits including health, local shops and 'social gains too'.

evidence.quote: There are social gains too

questions.text: Heading for Paragraph 5
options: An idea rooted in the cities of the past
The hidden price of car-centred design
The danger of benefits for the few
A question that outlives the practical details
questions.explanation: Paragraph 5 explains why the change is hard: 'reversing the pattern is slow and expensive'.

evidence.quote: reversing the pattern is slow and expensive

questions.text: Heading for Paragraph 6
options: An idea rooted in the cities of the past
The hidden price of car-centred design
The danger of benefits for the few
A question that outlives the practical details
correct_answer: The danger of benefits for the few
questions.explanation: Paragraph 6 warns the gains may be 'shared unequally' and walkability could become 'a luxury enjoyed mainly by the wealthy'.

evidence.quote: a luxury enjoyed mainly by the wealthy

questions.text: According to the passage, twentieth-century planners regarded building cities around cars as
questions.explanation: Paragraph 1 says planners 'regarded this transformation as obvious progress'.

evidence.quote: regarded this transformation as obvious progress

questions.text: In a fifteen-minute city, daily needs are met
options: within a short walk or cycle of home
correct_answer: within a short walk or cycle of home
questions.explanation: Paragraph 3 defines it as everything needed lying 'within a short walk or cycle of home'.

evidence.quote: within a short walk or cycle of home

questions.text: The passage suggests that streets busy with pedestrians
questions.explanation: Paragraph 4 states that streets busy with pedestrians 'tend to feel safer than those given over entirely to traffic'.

evidence.quote: tend to feel safer than those given over entirely to traffic

questions.text: What does the passage say about imposing walkable design without public support?
questions.explanation: Paragraph 5 says imposing it from above without persuasion 'is a reliable way to provoke opposition'.

evidence.quote: a reliable way to provoke opposition

questions.text: When a district becomes more attractive, ______ prices often rise.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 6 notes that when a district is made more attractive, 'property prices often rise'.

evidence.quote: property prices often rise

questions.text: Careful planners now treat ______ housing as essential to walkable design.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 6 says planners treat 'affordable housing as an essential companion to walkable design'.

evidence.quote: affordable housing as an essential companion to walkable design

questions.text: Which TWO cities are named as reclaiming road space for pedestrians and cyclists?
questions.explanation: Paragraph 7 names 'from Paris to Melbourne' as cities reclaiming road space.

evidence.quote: from Paris to Melbourne

sections.title: Passage 3 — Does Money Buy Happiness?
sections.instructions: You should spend about 20 minutes on Questions 27–40.
sections.passage:
Does Money Buy Happiness?

¶1 Few questions have attracted economists as persistently as whether money makes people happy. For most of the twentieth century the assumption went largely unexamined: wealthier was simply better, and the task of economic policy was to raise incomes as high as possible. Only in recent decades, as researchers began to measure well-being directly by asking people how satisfied they were with their lives, did the relationship between money and happiness reveal itself to be far more complicated than common sense had suggested. Happiness, the new data implied, was not a simple by-product of prosperity that economists could safely assume would rise in step with national output. Measuring it directly, rather than inferring it from how much people spent, opened a field of study that continues to unsettle some very comfortable assumptions.

¶2 The first puzzle to emerge became known as the Easterlin paradox, after the economist who described it. Within any single country at a given moment, richer individuals do report greater happiness than poorer ones. Yet as whole nations grew steadily wealthier over the decades, their citizens did not, on average, report becoming any happier. Income rose dramatically; reported satisfaction barely moved. This apparent contradiction suggested that what matters is not absolute wealth but how one's circumstances compare with those of others, or with one's own expectations. The comparison need not even be with the very rich; people seem to judge their lives against the neighbours they see, the colleagues they sit beside and the lifestyles they are shown on their screens every day. A rise in income that everyone enjoys at once may therefore leave each person feeling much as before.

¶3 Later studies have refined rather than overturned this picture. Researchers now distinguish between two kinds of well-being: the day-to-day emotional quality of a person's life, and the broader judgement they make when they evaluate their life as a whole. Money, it turns out, affects these differently. Professor Daniel Halloran has argued that rising income reliably improves people's overall evaluation of their lives, since wealth brings security and a sense of success. The moment-to-moment experience of happiness, however, appears to climb only until basic comfort is reached, after which additional income makes surprisingly little difference to how a person actually feels from hour to hour. The distinction matters for policy, because a government that aimed only to raise life evaluation might pursue very different measures from one that hoped to improve how its citizens feel in the ordinary moments of an ordinary day.

¶4 Why should extra money deliver such modest emotional returns? One influential explanation is the so-called hedonic treadmill. According to this idea, human beings adapt rapidly to improvements in their circumstances: a pay rise or a larger house brings a burst of pleasure that fades as the new situation comes to feel ordinary. Dr Mariko Sennan has shown that lottery winners, after an initial period of elation, tend within a year or two to report levels of happiness little different from before their win. We are, on this view, built to keep wanting more, which makes lasting contentment through consumption almost impossible to achieve. Advertising, several researchers note, both depends upon and reinforces this restlessness, continually presenting the next purchase as the one that will finally bring satisfaction. The treadmill, in short, is not merely a quirk of psychology but a force actively sustained by the economy that surrounds us.

¶5 Comparison with others plays an equally powerful role. Several experiments have found that people will accept a lower salary in absolute terms if it places them above their colleagues, rather than a higher salary that leaves them at the bottom of the office. Such findings trouble the traditional economic assumption that more is always preferred to less. They imply that a society in which everyone grew richer together might generate little additional happiness, because each person's relative position would remain unchanged. Wealth, in this sense, can behave less like food, which satisfies, than like status, which is forever contested. This helps to explain a result that puzzles newcomers to the field: rapid economic growth across a whole country can leave reported happiness almost flat, even as living standards visibly improve, because people's expectations tend to climb just as quickly as their incomes do.

¶6 If money buys less happiness than expected, how people spend it may matter more than how much they have. Research consistently suggests that spending on experiences, such as travel or concerts, yields more lasting satisfaction than spending on possessions, which quickly lose their novelty. Spending on others appears especially rewarding: in one well-known study, participants given a small sum to give away reported greater happiness than those told to spend it on themselves. Time, too, can be bought; people who use money to escape disliked chores tend to report higher satisfaction than those who simply accumulate goods. The pattern points towards a practical conclusion: if satisfaction rather than display is the goal, a society might do better to encourage shared experiences and generosity than to measure its success purely by the volume of goods its citizens manage to buy.

¶7 None of this implies that money is unimportant. For those struggling to afford food, housing or medical care, additional income transforms well-being, and to pretend otherwise would be both false and complacent. The lesson of the research is narrower but still significant: beyond the point of reasonable security, the pursuit of ever-greater wealth is a surprisingly unreliable route to a happier life. Whether societies that have organised themselves almost entirely around economic growth are prepared to absorb that lesson remains, for now, an open question. What is already clear is that the old equation of more money with more happiness, once treated as self-evident, no longer survives close contact with the evidence that researchers have so patiently gathered.

questions.text: For most of the twentieth century, economists rarely questioned the idea that higher income was better.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 1 says the assumption 'went largely unexamined: wealthier was simply better', agreeing with the statement.

evidence.quote: wealthier was simply better

questions.text: Within one country, richer people report being happier than poorer people.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 states that within a country 'richer individuals do report greater happiness than poorer ones'.

evidence.quote: richer individuals do report greater happiness than poorer ones

questions.text: As nations became wealthier, their citizens reported a large rise in happiness.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 says as nations grew wealthier their citizens 'did not, on average, report becoming any happier', contradicting a large rise.

evidence.quote: did not, on average, report becoming any happier

questions.text: The Easterlin paradox is now rejected by most economists.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 3 says later studies 'refined rather than overturned this picture', so it has not been rejected.

evidence.quote: refined rather than overturned this picture

questions.text: More people now measure well-being than measure income.
questions.explanation: The passage discusses measuring well-being but never compares how many people measure it against income, so this is Not Given.

questions.text: The writer believes money is unimportant to people living in poverty.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 7 says for those struggling to afford essentials, 'additional income transforms well-being', the opposite of unimportant.

evidence.quote: additional income transforms well-being

questions.text: According to Professor Halloran, rising income mainly improves
options: people's overall evaluation of their lives
correct_answer: people's overall evaluation of their lives
questions.explanation: Paragraph 3 says Halloran argues rising income 'reliably improves people's overall evaluation of their lives'.

evidence.quote: reliably improves people's overall evaluation of their lives

questions.text: The 'hedonic treadmill' refers to the tendency of people to
options: adapt rapidly to improvements in their circumstances
correct_answer: adapt rapidly to improvements in their circumstances
questions.explanation: Paragraph 4 explains the hedonic treadmill as the way people 'adapt rapidly to improvements in their circumstances'.

evidence.quote: adapt rapidly to improvements in their circumstances

questions.text: Experiments on salary and comparison suggest that people sometimes
options: prefer a lower salary that ranks above colleagues
correct_answer: prefer a lower salary that ranks above colleagues
questions.explanation: Paragraph 5 reports people will accept a lower salary 'if it places them above their colleagues'.

evidence.quote: if it places them above their colleagues

questions.text: Research on spending suggests the most lasting satisfaction comes from spending on
questions.explanation: Paragraph 6 says spending on experiences 'yields more lasting satisfaction than spending on possessions'.

evidence.quote: yields more lasting satisfaction than spending on possessions

questions.text: Lottery winners tend to return to their earlier level of happiness within a year or two.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 4 attributes the finding about lottery winners to 'Dr Mariko Sennan'.

evidence.quote: lottery winners, after an initial period of elation

questions.text: Wealth brings security and a sense of success, improving life evaluation.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 3: Halloran argues wealth brings 'security and a sense of success'.

evidence.quote: since wealth brings security and a sense of success

questions.text: In one study, what were participants given a small sum and told to do, which raised their happiness?
questions.explanation: Paragraph 6 describes participants 'given a small sum to give away' reporting greater happiness.

evidence.quote: given a small sum to give away

questions.text: Beyond the point of reasonable security, pursuing greater wealth is described as an unreliable route to a happier ______.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 7 concludes that beyond security the pursuit of wealth 'is a surprisingly unreliable route to a happier life'.

evidence.quote: a surprisingly unreliable route to a happier life
